tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: BILCAP (Primrose et al.)
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 51.1 months (capecitabine) vs 36.4 months (observation) in per-protocol analysis; ITT analysis median OS 49.6 vs 36.1 (adjusted HR 0.84; 95% CI 0.67–1.06)
trial_data.progression_free_survival: Median RFS 24.4 vs 17.5 months (HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.58–0.98; P = .033) in ITT
trial_data.hazard_ratio: OS HR 0.75 (95% CI 0.58–0.97; P = .028) per-protocol; protocol-specified sensitivity HR 0.74 (95% CI 0.59–0.94)
trial_data.notes: The phase III BILCAP study showed improved overall survival for adjuvant capecitabine in the per-protocol analysis; the ITT analysis did not reach statistical significance. Adjuvant therapy is recommended for up to 6 months. This is the only category 1 adjuvant regimen.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine monotherapy is the preferred adjuvant regimen for resected biliary tract cancers (gallbladder, intrahepatic, and extrahepatic). Based on the BILCAP trial, adjuvant therapy is given for up to 6 months. The benefit is most pronounced in the per-protocol population; the ITT analysis showed a non-significant trend. Adjuvant chemotherapy is associated with survival benefit, especially in lymph node-positive disease.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: SWOG S0809
trial_data.notes: This regimen is commonly followed by chemoradiation when the combination of chemotherapy and chemoradiation is recommended. Data come from a phase II trial in extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma and gallbladder cancer.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant capecitabine and gemcitabine is an other recommended regimen. In the SWOG S0809 trial, patients received 4 cycles of capecitabine/gemcitabine followed by concurrent capecitabine and radiotherapy. The regimen was generally tolerable with 86% completing therapy. This approach is used when chemoradiation is planned post-systemic therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.notes: If a patient is ineligible for cisplatin, carboplatin may be used (Williams KJ et al. HPB 2010;12:418-426). Data are extrapolated from the advanced disease setting; no phase III adjuvant trial specifically supports this regimen.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/gemcitabine is an other recommended adjuvant option. Although not specifically studied in the adjuvant setting, this regimen is commonly used based on efficacy in advanced disease and extrapolation from the ABC-02 trial. Carboplatin may be substituted if cisplatin is not tolerated.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent fluorouracil/leucovorin or gemcitabine are listed as other recommended adjuvant regimens. Data are limited; these are considered less preferred alternatives when capecitabine-based regimens are not suitable.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent gemcitabine is listed as an other recommended adjuvant regimen. A Japanese phase III trial (Ebata et al. Br J Surg 2018) showed no difference in survival compared to observation for resected bile duct cancer.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine is an agent used with concurrent radiation in the adjuvant setting. Typically given with RT at 45 Gy to regional lymph nodes and 50–60 Gy to tumor bed. Data from SWOG S0809 support this combination.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluorouracil is an alternative radiosensitizer for concurrent chemoradiation in the adjuvant setting. Used similarly to capecitabine with conventional fractionation RT.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: TOPAZ-1
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 12.9 vs 11.3 months (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.64–0.91) in updated analysis; initial OS HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.66–0.97; P = .021)
trial_data.notes: TOPAZ-1 randomized 685 patients to durvalumab + gemcitabine/cisplatin vs placebo + gemcitabine/cisplatin. Decreased neutrophil count was the most common grade 3/4 AE (21% durvalumab, 25% placebo).

tier_1_regimens.notes: This is a category 1 preferred first-line regimen for unresectable or metastatic BTC. Also recommended as a treatment option for patients who develop recurrent disease >6 months after curative surgery and >6 months after completing adjuvant therapy. Carboplatin may be substituted for cisplatin if needed. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV durvalumab? Actually footnote k applies to pembrolizumab. Durvalumab is IV only.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-966
trial_data.progression_free_survival: PFS HR 0.86 (95% CI 0.75–1.00; P = .023) at final analysis
trial_data.notes: KEYNOTE-966 randomized 1069 patients. The most common grade 3/4 AEs were similar (70% pembrolizumab, 69% placebo). Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection (with hyaluronidase) may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 preferred first-line option. Similar to TOPAZ-1 but with pembrolizumab. Carboplatin may replace cisplatin. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph SC may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: ABC-02 (Valle et al.)
trial_data.progression_free_survival: Median PFS 8.0 vs 5.0 months (HR 0.63; 95% CI 0.51–0.77; P < .001)
trial_data.notes: ABC-02 enrolled patients with locally advanced or metastatic CCA, gallbladder, and ampullary cancer. Carboplatin may be substituted if cisplatin ineligible.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 other recommended first-line regimen. This was the standard of care before immunotherapy combinations. Carboplatin is an acceptable substitute (phase II data show similar efficacy).

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Sahai et al. (JAMA Oncol 2018)
trial_data.notes: This phase II trial included patients with advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma. The regimen was well-tolerated.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Other recommended first-line regimen, particularly for cholangiocarcinoma. Data from a single-arm phase II trial. No direct comparison to cisplatin/gemcitabine.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Other recommended first-line regimen based on phase II data showing activity in advanced BTC. No phase III data specifically for this combination in first-line.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Kim ST et al. (Ann Oncol 2019)
trial_data.notes: A phase III non-inferiority trial showed capecitabine/oxaliplatin is non-inferior to gemcitabine/oxaliplatin for 6-month PFS.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Other recommended first-line fluoropyrimidine/platinum option. Considered when gemcitabine-based regimens are not preferred.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX is listed as an other recommended first-line option, but it is more commonly used in the second-line setting. Efficacy data in first-line are limited; extrapolated from second-line ABC-06 trial.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Gemcitabine/oxaliplatin (GEMOX) is an other recommended first-line regimen based on multiple phase II trials. A phase III trial (PRODIGE 12) in the adjuvant setting was negative.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent capecitabine is an option for patients who cannot tolerate combination chemotherapy. Data from phase II trials in advanced BTC show modest activity.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent fluorouracil/leucovorin is an option for patients unfit for combination therapy. Historically used based on EORTC 40955 trial.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent gemcitabine is an option for patients who cannot tolerate platinum doublets. Combination therapy is preferred based on ABC-02 results demonstrating superiority of cisplatin/gemcitabine over gemcitabine alone.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Integrated analysis of three phase 1-2 trials (Doebele et al. Lancet Oncol 2020)
trial_data.notes: Entrectinib is a TRK inhibitor approved for tumor-agnostic NTRK fusion-positive advanced solid tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of unresectable or metastatic NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive BTC. Do not use if progression on prior TRK inhibitors.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Drilon et al. N Engl J Med 2018
trial_data.notes: Larotrectinib was approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors irrespective of histology.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is a first-line option for NTRK fusion-positive BTC. Do not use after progression on prior TRK inhibitors.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: TRIDENT-1 (Besse et al. Nat Med 2026)
trial_data.overall_response_rate: 50% in TRK inhibitor-pretreated NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (abstract)
trial_data.notes: Repotrectinib is a next-generation TRK inhibitor approved for NTRK fusion-positive tumors after prior TRK inhibitor therapy.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is useful in certain circumstances for first-line (if no prior TRK inhibitor) or subsequent-line (if progression on prior TRK inhibitor). Different from entrectinib/larotrectinib: repotrectinib can be used after prior TRK inhibitors.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 23.5 months (all); CCA subgroup 24.3 months
trial_data.notes: FDA-approved tumor-agnostic for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors. Limited data in first-line BTC; caution.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of MSI-H/dMMR unresectable or metastatic BTC. Data are limited, especially in first-line. Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: ARROW (Subbiah et al. Nat Med 2022)
trial_data.notes: Two of three CCA patients responded. RET fusions are rare in BTC (<1%).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of RET fusion-positive BTC. Also an option in subsequent-line.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: LIBRETTO-001 (Subbiah et al. Lancet Oncol 2022)
trial_data.notes: Objective response was obtained in the one patient with CCA.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances first-line option for RET fusion-positive BTC. Also recommended for subsequent-line therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: eNRGy trial (Schram et al. N Engl J Med 2025; Mol Cancer Ther 2025)
trial_data.notes: Zenocutuzumab is a bispecific antibody targeting NRG1 fusions. NRG1 fusions are rare in CCA (<1%).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B for first-line treatment of CCA with NRG1 gene fusions. Also recommended as category 2A in subsequent-line.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 848 (Schenker et al. J Immunother Cancer 2024)
trial_data.notes: TMB-H defined as ≥10 mut/Mb by tissue or blood. No BTC-specific data; based on pan-tumor benefit.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of TMB-H unresectable or metastatic BTC. Also recommended in subsequent-line.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: ABC-06 (Lamarca et al. Lancet Oncol 2021)
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 6.2 vs 5.3 months (adjusted HR 0.69; P = .031)
trial_data.notes: FOLFOX vs active symptom control after progression on gemcitabine/cisplatin.

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX is the preferred subsequent-line systemic therapy for advanced BTC after progression on first-line chemotherapy. ABC-06 demonstrated a modest OS benefit over best supportive care.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Caparica et al. 2019; Mizrahi et al. 2020
trial_data.notes: Retrospective data show modest efficacy. No randomized data compared to FOLFOX.

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFIRI is an other recommended second-line option. A randomized phase II trial (Choi et al.) showed similar efficacy to FOLFOX.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: NIFTY (Hyung et al. JAMA Oncol 2023); AIO NALIRICC (Vogel et al. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol 2024)
trial_data.overall_survival: NIFTY: median OS 4.2 vs 1.7 mo (HR 0.61; P = .004) (updated); NALIRICC: no OS benefit (HR 1.08)
trial_data.progression_free_survival: NIFTY: PFS 7.1 vs 1.4 mo (HR 0.56) per BICR
trial_data.hazard_ratio: Pooled analysis (Yoo et al. J Hepatol 2025): HR 0.67 for PFS
trial_data.notes: NIFTY (South Korea) showed benefit; NALIRICC (Europe) did not. Pooled analysis suggests modest benefit. Category 2B due to conflicting data.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B other recommended second-line option. The NIFTY trial was positive, NALIRICC was negative. Treatment selection should consider patient factors and prior therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Sun et al. Cancer 2019; REACHIN (Demols et al. Ann Oncol 2020)
trial_data.notes: Multikinase inhibitor. Some disease control activity but no survival benefit in REACHIN.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B other recommended subsequent-line option. Limited efficacy data; may be considered for chemotherapy-refractory disease.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: Kim RD et al. JAMA Oncol 2020
trial_data.notes: Single-arm phase II in advanced refractory BTC. Nivolumab subcutaneous injection may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line therapy in patients not previously treated with a checkpoint inhibitor. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy SC may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: ROAR basket trial (Subbiah et al. Lancet Oncol 2020); NCI-MATCH Subprotocol H (Salama et al. J Clin Oncol 2020)
trial_data.progression_free_survival: ROAR: median PFS 9 months; NCI-MATCH: PFS 11.4 months
trial_data.notes: FDA-approved for BRAF V600E advanced solid tumors. BRAF V600E occurs in 1-5% of BTC.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line treatment of BRAF V600E-mutated BTC. BRAF V600E testing is recommended for all BTC subsites.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: DESTINY-PanTumor02 (Meric-Bernstam et al. J Clin Oncol 2024)
trial_data.notes: HER2 IHC3+ required. Significant heterogeneity in HER2 testing in BTC; no standardized cutoffs.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for HER2-positive (IHC3+) advanced BTC after prior therapy. HER2 testing is recommended for all BTC subsites.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: MyPathway (Javle et al. Lancet Oncol 2021); TAPUR (Cannon et al. J Clin Oncol 2024)
trial_data.notes: Both studies included HER2-amplified/overexpressed BTC. Pertuzumab/trastuzumab is a dual HER2 blockade.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for HER2-positive BTC. Requires IHC3+ or ISH+/NGS amplification.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: SGNTUC-019 (Nakamura et al. J Clin Oncol 2023)
trial_data.notes: Tucatinib is a HER2-selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor used with trastuzumab.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for HER2-positive advanced BTC. Patient population required IHC3+/ISH+/NGS+.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: HERIZON-BTC-01 (Harding et al. Lancet Oncol 2023)
trial_data.overall_response_rate: 41.3% (IHC2+/3+); 51.6% in IHC3+ subgroup; disease control rate 68.8%
trial_data.notes: Zanidatamab is a bispecific antibody targeting HER2. Responses seen across all BTC subtypes.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for HER2-amplified (IHC3+) advanced BTC after prior gemcitabine-based therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: KRYSTAL-1 (Bekaii-Saab et al. J Clin Oncol 2023)
trial_data.notes: KRAS G12C occurs in ~1% of BTC. Adagrasib is FDA-approved for KRAS G12C advanced solid tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line treatment of KRAS G12C-mutated BTC. Testing for KRAS G12C is recommended.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: FOENIX-CA2 (Goyal et al. N Engl J Med 2023)
trial_data.notes: Futibatinib is a covalent FGFR1-4 inhibitor. Preferred over erdafitinib.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for CCA with FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements in subsequent-line. Testing recommended for iCCA and eCCA; should be considered for gallbladder cancer. Futibatinib is preferred over erdafitinib.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: FIGHT-202 (Abou-Alfa et al. Lancet Oncol 2020; Vogel et al. ESMO Open 2024)
trial_data.notes: Pemigatinib is a selective FGFR1-3 inhibitor. FDA-approved for previously treated FGFR2-rearranged CCA.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for FGFR2 fusion-positive CCA. Preferred over erdafitinib.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

biomarkers.marker: FGFR2 gene fusion/rearrangement or other FGFR alterations

trial_data.trial_name: RAGNAR (Pant et al. Lancet Oncol 2023)
trial_data.notes: Erdafitinib is a pan-FGFR inhibitor. Data from a smaller trial compared to futibatinib/pemigatinib.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for FGFR2 fusion-positive CCA, but futibatinib and pemigatinib are preferred. Erdafitinib has a smaller evidence base.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: ClarIDHy (Abou-Alfa et al. Lancet Oncol 2020; Zhu et al. JAMA Oncol 2021)
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 10.3 vs 7.5 months (HR 0.79; 95% CI 0.56-1.12; P = .09) ITT; after crossover adjustment HR 0.49 (95% CI 0.34-0.70; P < .001)
trial_data.notes: IDH1 mutations occur in 10-20% of iCCA. Ivosidenib is a category 1 recommendation.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line treatment of IDH1-mutated CCA. Testing for IDH1 is recommended for iCCA and eCCA; should be considered for gallbladder cancer.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158 updated analysis (Maio et al. Ann Oncol 2022)
trial_data.notes: Updated analysis confirmed durable responses. Pembrolizumab is tumor-agnostic approved.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line treatment of MSI-H/dMMR BTC without prior checkpoint inhibitor. Pembrolizumab SC may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: GARNET (André et al. JAMA Netw Open 2023)
trial_data.notes: Dostarlimab is an anti-PD-1 antibody approved for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances for MSI-H/dMMR BTC without prior checkpoint inhibitor and no satisfactory alternative. Recommended for patients who have progressed on prior treatment.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 848 (Schenker et al. J Immunother Cancer 2024)
trial_data.notes: For patients with disease refractory to standard therapies or no standard options.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line treatment of TMB-H BTC without prior checkpoint inhibitor. Nivolumab SC may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.site: Gallbladder cancer, Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158 TMB cohort (Marabelle et al. Lancet Oncol 2020)
trial_data.n_patients: 102 TMB-H (none BTC in cohort, but pan-tumor benefit)
trial_data.notes: FDA-approved for TMB-H advanced solid tumors after prior treatment with no satisfactory alternatives.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances for TMB-H BTC without prior checkpoint inhibitor. TMB testing should be performed using a clinically validated MGPT.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma

trial_data.trial_name: eNRGy trial (Schram et al. N Engl J Med 2025; Mol Cancer Ther 2025)
trial_data.notes: NRG1 fusions are rare in CCA. RNA-based MGPT is preferred for detection.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2A useful in certain circumstances for subsequent-line therapy of NRG1 fusion-positive CCA. Category 2B in first-line.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Mixed HCC-CCA: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Durvalumab or Pembrolizumab
tier_1_regimens.site: Mixed HCC-CCA (combined hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Retrospective series (Pomej et al. ESMO Open 2023); extrapolated from TOPAZ-1 (CCA) and HCC trials
trial_data.n_patients: 101 (retrospective); no prospective trial specifically for mixed HCC-CCA
trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 15.5 months (chemotherapy) vs 5.3 months (non-chemotherapy) P = .052 (retrospective)
trial_data.notes: No prospective data for the combination. Based on activity of chemotherapy and immunotherapy in both HCC and CCA components.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For advanced mixed HCC-CCA, cisplatin/gemcitabine combined with durvalumab or pembrolizumab is an appropriate first-line choice. These combinations include agents with activity in both CCA and HCC. Pembrolizumab SC may be substituted.

tier_1_regimens.site: Mixed HCC-CCA

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 040 (HCC) and Klein et al. JAMA Oncol 2020 (BTC subgroup)
trial_data.notes: Ipilimumab + nivolumab has demonstrated activity in both HCC and CCA. Recommended in absence of a targetable aberration.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Reasonable option for mixed HCC-CCA at progression if no targetable aberration. Activity shown in both HCC and CCA.

tier_1_regimens.site: Mixed HCC-CCA

trial_data.trial_name: RESORCE (HCC) and Sun et al. Cancer 2019 (BTC)
trial_data.overall_survival: RESORCE: median OS 10.6 vs 7.8 months (HR 0.63); Sun: median OS 5.3 months (BTC)
trial_data.notes: Regorafenib has activity in both HCC and CCA. Reasonable option for mixed HCC-CCA without targetable alteration.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regorafenib is an option for mixed HCC-CCA after progression if no targetable aberration. Based on activity in both HCC (RESORCE) and BTC (phase II).

tier_2_handbook.gallbladder_cancer_definition: Gallbladder cancer is the most common anatomic subtype of biliary tract cancer (BTC), arising from the epithelial lining of the gallbladder [MS-3]. The vast majority of gallbladder cancers are adenocarcinomas [MS-3]. The disease is characterized by local and vascular invasion, regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastases [MS-3]. Gallbladder cancer is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage due to its asymptomatic early course and presentation mimicking benign biliary conditions; it is often discovered incidentally at cholecystectomy for presumed benign disease or on pathologic review [MS-4]. Clinically, patients may present with biliary obstruction and jaundice, chronic right upper quadrant pain, or a suspicious mass on imaging. The presence of jaundice portends a poor prognosis, with advanced disease in 96% of such cases and significantly lower disease-specific survival [MS-4]. Embryologically, the gallbladder derives from the foregut endoderm, and the cancer typically arises in the fundus, body, or neck of the gallbladder. Anatomically, the gallbladder is a pear-shaped organ beneath the liver; its proximity to the liver and biliary tree facilitates direct invasion and spread. Staging is based on tumor depth (T1–T4), nodal involvement (N0–N2), and distant metastasis (M0–M1) per AJCC 8th edition [ST-1].
tier_2_handbook.gallbladder_cancer_subtypes: The predominant histologic type of gallbladder cancer is adenocarcinoma, accounting for the vast majority of cases [MS-3]. Other rare histologies include neuroendocrine tumors, sarcomas, and squamous cell carcinomas; however, these are not staged as gallbladder carcinoma and should be managed according to relevant NCCN Guidelines for those tumor types [GALL-B]. Within adenocarcinoma, there are no standardized molecular subtypes recognized for routine clinical use, but molecular profiling reveals distinct genomic alterations (see molecular pathogenesis). Pathologic staging parameters include carcinoma in situ/high-grade dysplasia, tumor depth of invasion (T stage), number of regional lymph nodes involved (N stage), and distant metastasis (M stage) [GALL-B]. Histologic differentiation (well, moderate, poorly differentiated) is reported but not subtype-specific. The incidence of microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) is approximately 1%–3% [BIL-B 3/8]. Gallbladder cancers with MSI-H are more prevalent in patients with chronic cholecystitis or pancreaticobiliary maljunction [MS-3].
tier_2_handbook.gallbladder_cancer_molecular_pathogenesi: Genomic analysis of gallbladder cancer has identified clinically relevant alterations, though the landscape is less well characterized than that of cholangiocarcinoma. HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification is present in approximately 15%–30% of gallbladder cancers [BIL-B 3/8; MS-30]. Activating mutations in BRAF V600E occur in 1%–5% of cases [BIL-B 3/8]. FGFR2 gene fusions and IDH1 mutations are rare in gallbladder cancer but testing may be considered in select cases [BIL-B 2/8]. NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions are exceedingly rare (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. MSI-H/dMMR is found in 1%–3% of tumors, and TMB-H in <5% [BIL-B 3/8]. KRAS G12C mutations occur in approximately 1% of gallbladder cancers [BIL-B 3/8]. RET gene fusions are also rare (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. The genomic profile suggests potential for targeted therapy with agents approved in other tumor types, such as trastuzumab-based combinations for HER2-positive disease, dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E, and immune checkpoint inhibitors for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors [BIL-C 3/5]. Germline pathogenic variants (e.g., BRCA2, BRCA1, MLH1, MSH2) are found in approximately 9%–11% of BTC patients, with gallbladder cancer included in these studies [BIL-B 1/8].
tier_2_handbook.gallbladder_cancer_epidemiology: In the United States, an estimated 12,610 new cases of gallbladder cancer and other biliary tract cancers are expected in 2025, with approximately 4,400 deaths [MS-2]. Gallbladder cancer is the most common anatomic subtype of BTC [MS-3]. Incidence increases steadily with age, and females are more likely to be diagnosed than males [MS-3]. Incidence and mortality rates are highest among American Indian, Alaska Native, and Asian/Pacific Islander populations [MS-3]. From 1973 to 2015, the incidence of gallbladder cancer decreased in both sexes, and in white, American Indian/Alaska Native, and Asian/Pacific Islander individuals, but showed a small trend toward increase in Black individuals [MS-3]. Globally, there are pockets of elevated incidence in Korea, Japan, parts of Eastern Europe, South America (especially Bolivia, Chile, Spain), and in females in India, Pakistan, and Ecuador [MS-3]. The 5-year overall survival for all stages is poor; median survival for the entire cohort of 435 patients at a single center was 10.3 months, with 12.9 months for stage IA–III and 5.8 months for stage IV [MS-3–MS-4]. Tumor stage is the strongest prognostic factor [MS-3].
tier_2_handbook.gallbladder_cancer_risk_factors: The most prevalent risk factor for gallbladder cancer is cholelithiasis with chronic inflammation; risk increases with stone size [MS-3]. Calcification of the gallbladder wall (porcelain gallbladder), a consequence of chronic inflammation, has historically been associated with elevated risk, with cancer present in 7%–15% of such patients [MS-3]. Other established risk factors include anomalous pancreaticobiliary duct junction, gallbladder polyps >1 cm, chronic typhoid infection, primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), and adenomyomatosis of the gallbladder [MS-3]. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is considered beneficial for high-risk patients (e.g., porcelain gallbladder, polyps >1 cm) [MS-3]. Patients with a history of chronic cholecystitis or pancreaticobiliary maljunction have a greater prevalence of MSI-H gallbladder cancers [MS-3]. Risk factors are largely inflammatory in nature, and most gallbladder cancers are sporadic; however, up to 10%–15% of BTCs may be associated with an inherited cancer predisposition syndrome (e.g., BRCA2, BRCA1, Lynch syndrome) [BIL-B 1/8].
tier_2_handbook.intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_definiti: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) is a primary liver cancer arising from the epithelium of the intrahepatic bile ducts within the hepatic parenchyma, also referred to as peripheral cholangiocarcinoma [MS-9; Figure 1]. It is a highly lethal malignancy, and its incidence has been rising dramatically in recent decades [MS-9]. iCCA accounts for approximately 10%–20% of primary liver cancers and is the second most common primary hepatic malignancy after hepatocellular carcinoma. More than 90% of iCCAs are adenocarcinomas [MS-9]. Morphologically, iCCA is classified into three growth patterns: mass-forming (most common), periductal-infiltrating, and intraductal-growing [MS-9; INTRA-C]. Histologically, iCCA is further categorized as small duct type or large duct type based on differentiation and cell morphology [INTRA-C]. The diagnosis is based on histologic confirmation demonstrating cholangiocyte differentiation, supported by immunohistochemistry and albumin in situ hybridization when necessary [INTRA-C]. iCCA typically presents with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, weight loss, abdominal pain, and is often detected as an isolated hepatic mass on imaging [MS-11]. Biliary obstruction is uncommon unless the tumor involves the hepatic hilum. Staging follows the AJCC 8th edition classification for intrahepatic bile duct tumors, which considers tumor number, size, vascular invasion, lymph node involvement, and distant metastasis [ST-2].
tier_2_handbook.intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_subtypes: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma is classified morphologically into mass-forming, periductal-infiltrating, and intraductal-growing types [MS-9]. The mass-forming type appears as a multinodular distinct mass with malignant glands in a sclerotic stroma and well-demarcated borders. Periductal-infiltrating type is characterized by poorly defined borders and a linear growth pattern along an intermediate or larger native bile duct [INTRA-C]. Intraductal-growing type is less common. Histologically, iCCA is divided into small duct type and large duct type based on differentiation and cytokeratin expression [INTRA-C]. Molecular subtypes are increasingly recognized: approximately 9%–15% of iCCAs harbor FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements, and 10%–20% have IDH1 mutations [BIL-B 3/8]. Other subtypes include those with BRAF V600E mutations (1%–5%), HER2 alterations (5%–20%), MSI-H (1%–3%), TMB-H (<5%), and rare NTRK fusions (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. Mixed HCC-CCA is a distinct entity (1%–10% of primary liver tumors) with both hepatocellular and cholangiocytic components [INTRA-B]. The subtype classification has therapeutic implications, as FGFR2 fusions and IDH1 mutations are actionable targets with approved therapies.
tier_2_handbook.intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_molecula: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma harbors a distinct genomic landscape with frequent alterations in IDH1 (10%–20% of cases) and FGFR2 gene fusions/rearrangements (9%–15% of cases) [BIL-B 3/8]. These alterations are relatively specific to iCCA and rare in other biliary tract subsites. BRAF V600E mutations occur in 1%–5% of iCCA [BIL-B 3/8]. HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification is present in 5%–20% of iCCA [BIL-B 3/8]. Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) is found in 1%–3%, and TMB-high in <5% [BIL-B 3/8]. NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions are exceedingly rare (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. RET gene fusions are also rare (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. KRAS G12C mutations occur in approximately 1% of iCCA [BIL-B 3/8]. NRG1 gene fusions are identified in <1% of iCCA [BIL-B 3/8]. For mixed HCC-CCA, genomic profiling reveals a higher prevalence of TP53 and TERT promoter mutations, more commonly associated with HCC, particularly in patients with underlying hepatitis C virus infection [INTRA-B 1/2]. IDH1 mutations in iCCA most commonly occur at codon 132 (R132X) [BIL-B 5/8]. These genomic alterations define potential targets for therapy, including FGFR inhibitors (pemigatinib, futibatinib, erdafitinib), IDH1 inhibitor (ivosidenib), and others [BIL-C 3/5].
tier_2_handbook.intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_epidemio: In the United States, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) is estimated to account for a significant proportion of the 42,240 new liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancer cases expected in 2025, with approximately 30,090 deaths attributed to liver/intrahepatic bile duct cancer [MS-2]. From 2001 to 2017, the incidence of iCCA increased dramatically by 148.8%, while extrahepatic CCA increased at a slower rate of 7.5% [MS-9]. The increase in incidence may be partly attributable to improved diagnostic accuracy [MS-9]. Five-year overall survival rates for iCCA improved from 1973 to 2008, likely due to advances in treatment [MS-9]. The median survival for patients with unresectable disease is poor; the ABC-02 trial showed median overall survival of 11.7 months with gemcitabine/cisplatin [MS-23]. iCCA occurs more frequently in patients with underlying liver disease, including cirrhosis, hepatitis B and C, and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) [MS-9–MS-10]. Racial and ethnic variation exists but specific incidence rates by demographic for iCCA alone are less commonly reported. The disease often presents at an advanced stage, with only 20%–30% of patients eligible for curative resection at diagnosis.
tier_2_handbook.intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_risk_fac: Risk factors for intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma are associated with chronic inflammation and are similar to those for hepatocellular carcinoma. Strong risk factors include primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), hepatolithiasis (calculi of intrahepatic bile ducts), choledochal cysts, and liver fluke infections (Opisthorchis viverrini, Clonorchis sinensis) [MS-9]. Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection, hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection, cirrhosis, diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, and tobacco smoking are established risk factors for iCCA [MS-9]. A systematic review and meta-analysis identified biliary cysts/stones, cirrhosis, HBV, and HCV as the strongest risk factors for both intrahepatic and extrahepatic CCA [MS-9]. Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is associated with increased incidence of iCCA (pooled adjusted OR 2.09; 95% CI 1.49–2.91) [MS-10]. Unlike gallbladder cancer, cholelithiasis is not considered a risk factor for iCCA [MS-9]. Inflammatory bowel disease may increase risk, although this association is confounded by PSC [MS-9].
tier_2_handbook.extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_definiti: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) encompasses malignant tumors arising from the epithelium of the extrahepatic bile ducts, extending from the junction of the right and left hepatic ducts to the ampulla of Vater. It is subclassified into perihilar (hilar) and distal cholangiocarcinoma [MS-9]. Perihilar cholangiocarcinoma, also known as Klatskin tumor, occurs at or near the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts and the common hepatic duct; distal cholangiocarcinoma arises in the common bile duct above the ampulla of Vater [MS-9]. Perihilar tumors are the most common type of eCCA [MS-9]. More than 90% of eCCAs are adenocarcinomas [MS-9]. Patients typically present with obstructive jaundice, pruritus, and sometimes abdominal pain. Diagnosis is confirmed by histology demonstrating cholangiocyte differentiation, supported by immunohistochemistry and albumin in situ hybridization [EXTRA-B]. Anatomically, perihilar tumors are staged using the AJCC perihilar bile duct classification, while distal tumors use the distal bile duct staging system [ST-3, ST-4]. The modified Bismuth-Corlette and Blumgart staging systems are also used for perihilar tumors to assess resectability and prognosis [MS-10–MS-11]. Complete surgical resection with negative margins is the only potentially curative treatment, but many patients present with advanced disease [MS-17].
tier_2_handbook.extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_subtypes: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma is anatomically divided into perihilar (hilar) and distal subtypes. Perihilar tumors arise in the main lobar ducts proximal to the cystic duct; distal tumors arise in the extrahepatic biliary tree between the cystic duct confluence and the ampulla of Vater [EXTRA-B]. These two subtypes differ in surgical approach: perihilar tumors require major hepatic resection with caudate lobectomy, while distal tumors are managed by pancreaticoduodenectomy [EXTRA-A]. Histologically, both are predominantly adenocarcinomas. Growth patterns may be mass-forming, periductal-infiltrating, or intraductal, though classification by growth pattern is less commonly applied in eCCA. Pathologic parameters include depth of invasion (for distal tumors: <5 mm, 5–12 mm, >12 mm), lymph node involvement (N1: 1–3 positive nodes; N2: ≥4 positive nodes), and margin status [EXTRA-B]. Rare histologies such as neuroendocrine tumors or sarcomas are staged separately and should be managed according to relevant NCCN Guidelines [EXTRA-B]. Molecularly, eCCA has a lower frequency of FGFR2 fusions and IDH1 mutations compared to iCCA, but HER2 alterations (5%–20%) and other markers occur [BIL-B 2/8, 3/8].
tier_2_handbook.extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_molecula: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) has a distinct molecular profile from intrahepatic CCA. FGFR2 gene fusions/rearrangements and IDH1 mutations are rare in eCCA but testing is recommended due to potential therapeutic implications [BIL-B 2/8, 5/8]. HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification occurs in 5%–20% of eCCA [BIL-B 3/8]. BRAF V600E mutations are found in 1%–5% [BIL-B 3/8]. MSI-H/dMMR is present in 1%–3%, and TMB-H in <5% [BIL-B 3/8]. NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions are very rare (<1%) [BIL-B 3/8]. RET gene fusions occur in <1% [BIL-B 3/8]. KRAS G12C mutations are found in approximately 1% [BIL-B 3/8]. NRG1 gene fusions are rare (<1%) and have been reported in eCCA [BIL-B 2/8, 3/8]. Comprehensive genomic profiling is recommended for unresectable or metastatic eCCA to identify actionable alterations [BIL-B 1/8]. The genomic landscape also includes alterations in TP53, CDKN2A, and SMAD4, though these are not included in Table 1 of the guideline. Germline pathogenic variants (e.g., BRCA2, BRCA1, MLH1, MSH2, PALB2, RAD51D, BAP1, ATM) are present in approximately 9%–11% of BTCs including eCCA [BIL-B 1/8].
tier_2_handbook.extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_epidemio: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) is more common than intrahepatic CCA in the United States [MS-9]. From 2001 to 2017, the incidence of eCCA increased at a slower rate of 7.5% [MS-9]. SEER data analysis indicates that perihilar cholangiocarcinoma is the most common type of eCCA [MS-9]. The median overall survival for patients with unresectable eCCA is short; with supportive care it is measured in months, and with systemic therapy, median survival is approximately 1 year (11.7 months in the ABC-02 trial for advanced BTC including eCCA) [MS-23]. Five-year survival rates following complete resection range from 20%–42% for hilar CCA and 16%–52% for distal CCA [MS-17]. Surgical margin status, lymph node involvement, and depth of tumor invasion are independent predictors of survival [MS-17]. eCCA tends to present at a stage where resectability is often limited. Racial/ethnic variations are less well studied, but risk factors such as primary sclerosing cholangitis and parasitic infections contribute to geographic differences in incidence. The overall prognosis remains poor, and most patients ultimately die of their disease.
tier_2_handbook.extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_risk_fac: Established risk factors for extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma include primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), hepatolithiasis, choledochal cysts, and liver fluke infections [MS-9]. PSC is a particularly strong risk factor, and patients with PSC should be considered for surveillance. Chronic calculi of the bile duct (hepatolithiasis) are also well-established. Choledochal cysts, congenital dilations of the bile ducts, confer a significantly increased risk. Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) may be a risk factor, though this is often confounded by PSC [MS-9]. Other risk factors include hepatitis B virus (HBV) and hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection, cirrhosis, diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, and tobacco smoking, although these are more strongly linked to intrahepatic CCA [MS-9]. Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is associated with increased incidence of eCCA (pooled adjusted OR 2.05; 95% CI 1.59–2.64) [MS-10]. Unlike gallbladder cancer, cholelithiasis is not thought to be linked with eCCA [MS-9]. Most patients diagnosed with eCCA have no identifiable predisposing factors [MS-9].
tier_2_handbook.mixed_hcc_cca_definition: Mixed hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma (HCC-CCA) is a rare primary liver malignancy that contains histologic components of both hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and cholangiocarcinoma (CCA). It is estimated to represent 1% to 10% of primary liver tumors [INTRA-B 1/2]. The diagnosis is made on pathologic review, which may reveal either separate foci of HCC and CCA within the same tumor (combined type) or biphenotypic cells that coexpress immunohistochemical markers of both hepatocellular and cholangiocytic differentiation [INTRA-B 1/2]. Due to the dual histology, the clinical behavior of mixed HCC-CCA is often aggressive, and treatment strategies are evolving. The NCCN Guidelines emphasize that liver resection is the standard treatment for resectable disease [INTRA-B 1/2]. For unresectable disease, liver-directed locoregional therapies may be considered, similar to those used for HCC and iCCA [INTRA-B 1/2]. Liver transplantation may be evaluated in selected patients with limited tumor burden based on center-specific criteria [INTRA-B 1/2]. At advanced stages, biopsy at recurrence is recommended to determine the dominant histology, which may guide systemic therapy selection [INTRA-B 1/2].
tier_2_handbook.mixed_hcc_cca_subtypes: Mixed HCC-CCA is broadly divided into two histologic patterns: 1) Tumors with separate foci of HCC and CCA in discrete areas (combined type), and 2) Biphenotypic tumors where individual cells coexpress immunohistochemical markers of both HCC (e.g., Hep Par1, GPC3, AFP) and CCA (e.g., CK7, CK19) [INTRA-B 1/2]. This distinction has implications for diagnosis, as molecular profiling may show a predominance of HCC-associated genomic alterations (TP53, TERT promoter mutations) in tumors from patients with underlying hepatitis C virus infection [INTRA-B 1/2]. The identification of a targetable alteration associated with CCA (e.g., FGFR2 fusion, IDH1 mutation) should prompt consideration of appropriate targeted therapy [INTRA-B 2/2]. The subtype may change during disease progression; therefore, repeat biopsy at recurrence is recommended to reassess dominant histology, especially if there are discordant responses [INTRA-B 1/2].
tier_2_handbook.mixed_hcc_cca_molecular_pathogenesis: Genomic profiling of mixed HCC-CCA suggests a higher prevalence of mutations typically associated with HCC, including TP53 and TERT promoter mutations, particularly in patients with hepatitis C virus infection [INTRA-B 1/2]. These findings are based on small sample sizes and may not be generalizable to all mixed HCC-CCA cases. The presence of CCA-associated genomic alterations (e.g., FGFR2 fusions, IDH1 mutations, BRAF V600E, HER2 amplification) is less well characterized but could be identified using multigene panel testing (MGPT) [INTRA-B 2/2]. Because the tumor may harbor targetable aberrations, MGPT is recommended for all patients with advanced mixed HCC-CCA [INTRA-B 1/2]. At progression, molecularly targeted therapies directed against identified alterations (e.g., FGFR inhibitors, IDH1 inhibitor, BRAF/MEK inhibitors, HER2-directed agents) should be considered [INTRA-B 2/2; BIL-C 3/5]. In the absence of a targetable alteration, regimens with demonstrated activity in both HCC and CCA are options, including nivolumab plus ipilimumab or regorafenib [INTRA-B 1/2]. The genomic complexity and dual lineage contribute to the challenging management of this entity.
tier_2_handbook.mixed_hcc_cca_epidemiology: Mixed HCC-CCA is a rare primary liver tumor, estimated to represent 1% to 10% of all primary liver cancers [INTRA-B 1/2]. There are limited large-scale epidemiological data specific to this entity, as it is often grouped with either HCC or CCA in population-based studies. The incidence may be higher in patients with underlying chronic liver disease, particularly hepatitis C, where genomic profiling reveals more HCC-like alterations [INTRA-B 1/2]. A retrospective multicenter study including 101 patients reported a median overall survival of 15.5 months for those receiving chemotherapy, with a trend toward better survival compared to non-chemotherapy regimens [INTRA-B 1/2]. The 5-year overall survival after surgical resection varies, but recurrence is common and long-term outcomes are poor. Due to its rarity, standardized reporting and clinical trials are lacking, and most data come from small retrospective series. The diagnosis is often made incidentally at pathology review of a resected liver mass that was initially suspected to be either HCC or CCA.
tier_2_handbook.mixed_hcc_cca_risk_factors: Specific risk factors for mixed HCC-CCA are not well defined, but likely overlap with those for HCC and intrahepatic CCA. Underlying cirrhosis from any cause (viral hepatitis, alcohol, metabolic dysfunction) is a plausible risk factor. Hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection appears to be particularly associated with mixed HCC-CCA that harbors a higher prevalence of TP53 and TERT promoter mutations [INTRA-B 1/2]. Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection is also a risk factor. Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) may contribute. Other risk factors that are classic for CCA (e.g., primary sclerosing cholangitis, hepatolithiasis, liver flukes) have not been specifically confirmed for mixed HCC-CCA. Given its rarity, no large-scale epidemiological studies have identified unique risk factors. Patients with known chronic liver disease who develop a primary liver tumor with atypical imaging features should be considered for biopsy to evaluate for mixed histology.

gallbladder_cancer_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Gallbladder cancer is commonly diagnosed at an advanced stage because it is often asymptomatic in its early stages and has an aggressive nature that can spread rapidly. Another factor contributing to late diagnosis is a clinical presentation that mimics biliary colic or chronic cholecystitis. Hence, it is common for a diagnosis of gallbladder cancer to be an incidental finding at cholecystectomy for presumed benign gallbladder disease or, more frequently, on pathologic review following cholecystectomy for symptomatic cholelithiasis. In a retrospective review of 435 patients diagnosed and treated with curative resection at a single center from 1995 to 2005, 123 patients (47%) were diagnosed with gallbladder cancer as an incidental finding after cholecystectomy [23]. Other possible clinical presentations include a suspicious mass detected on ultrasound or biliary tract obstruction with jaundice or chronic right upper quadrant abdominal pain. The presence of jaundice in patients with gallbladder cancer is associated with a poor prognosis; patients with jaundice are more likely to have advanced-stage disease (96% vs. 60%; P < .001) and significantly lower disease-specific survival (6 vs. 16 months; P < .0001) than those without jaundice [25]. In a sample of 82 patients with gallbladder cancer who presented with jaundice, the resectability rate was low (7%), with even fewer having negative surgical margins (5%) and no disease-free survivors at 2 years [25].

intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_clinical.typical_presentation: Patients with intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) often present with nonspecific symptoms due to late diagnosis. Early-stage iCCA may manifest as mild changes in serum liver function tests, particularly alkaline phosphatase. Common symptoms include fever, weight loss, and/or abdominal pain; symptoms of biliary obstruction are uncommon because these tumors do not necessarily involve the common hepatic/bile duct. iCCA may be detected incidentally as an isolated intrahepatic mass on imaging performed for other reasons [85]. In a retrospective analysis, the 5-year survival rate after complete resection ranged from 20% to 40% depending on stage [75,77]. The incidence of iCCA has increased dramatically (148.8% from 2001 to 2017) likely due to improved diagnostic capabilities [65].

extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_clinical.typical_presentation: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) includes perihilar (Klatskin) and distal subtypes. Patients typically present with jaundice, which is the most common symptom, followed by evidence of biliary obstruction or abnormality on imaging. Pain, abnormal liver function tests, and pruritus are also common. Perihilar tumors often present with obstructive jaundice and may be associated with pruritus and dark urine. Distal cholangiocarcinomas also present with jaundice and may cause pancreatic duct obstruction leading to pancreatitis. Most CCAs (>90%) are adenocarcinomas [63]. Extrahepatic CCA is more common than intrahepatic CCA [64]. The 5-year survival rates following complete resection range from 20% to 42% for hilar and 16% to 52% for distal CCAs [198,199].

staging_pearls: Perihilar and distal bile duct tumors have separate AJCC staging systems per 8th edition [19].
N staging uses number of positive nodes (N1: 1-3, N2: ≥4) for both perihilar and distal [19].
Depth of invasion is incorporated into T staging for distal tumors (<5 mm, 5-12 mm, >12 mm) and is an independent predictor of outcome [80,81].
The modified Bismuth-Corlette and Blumgart staging systems are used for hilar CCA classification [82,83].
Transperitoneal biopsy is contraindicated in transplant candidates with perihilar CCA [90].
Liver transplantation may be considered for highly selected patients with ≤3 cm radial diameter, no metastases, and no nodal disease, with an UNOS-approved protocol.

mixed_hcc_cca_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Mixed hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma (HCC-CCA) is a rare primary liver tumor, estimated to occur in 1% to 10% of patients with primary liver tumors [181-184]. Tumors may contain separate foci of both HCC and CCA histology in discrete areas, or be biphenotypic with coexpression of markers. Patients often present with features of both HCC and CCA, such as a liver mass with elevated alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and CA 19-9, and may have underlying chronic liver disease (hepatitis B/C, cirrhosis, MASLD). The presentation can overlap with either pure HCC or CCA; diagnosis is made on pathology (biopsy or resection). Genomic profiling shows higher prevalence of TP53 and TERT promoter mutations similar to HCC, especially in HCV infection [3,5].

Liver resection is standard for resectable disease; locoregional therapies may be used for unresectable hepatic-limited disease, similar to HCC and iCCA management.
Transplant may be considered for selected patients with limited size per center criteria.
At recurrence, repeat biopsy to ascertain dominant histology is recommended to guide therapy.

management_principles.overview: Biliary tract cancers (BTCs) encompass gallbladder cancer and intrahepatic and extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (CCA). These are highly lethal malignancies, with an estimated 42,240 new cases of liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancer and 12,610 new cases of gallbladder and other BTCs expected in the United States in 2025 (Siegel et al. 2025). Approximately 30,090 deaths from liver/intrahepatic bile duct cancer and 4,400 deaths from gallbladder/other BTC are anticipated. The treatment philosophy emphasizes a multimodal approach integrating surgery, systemic therapy, radiation therapy, and locoregional therapies, guided by multidisciplinary evaluation. Complete resection with negative margins remains the only potentially curative treatment, but most patients present with advanced disease. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is considered for locoregionally advanced gallbladder cancer to rule out rapid progression and avoid futile surgery, though data are limited. Adjuvant chemotherapy (capecitabine category 1) or chemoradiation is recommended for resected disease, especially with lymph node involvement. For unresectable or metastatic disease, first-line systemic therapy includes durvalumab plus gemcitabine/cisplatin (category 1) or pembrolizumab plus gemcitabine/cisplatin (category 1), with targeted therapy options for biomarker-selected patients. The panel encourages clinical trial participation at all stages.

by_intent.patient_group: Resectable gallbladder cancer, intrahepatic CCA, extrahepatic CCA
by_intent.approach: Surgical resection with negative margins + regional lymphadenectomy; consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for locoregionally advanced gallbladder cancer; adjuvant capecitabine (category 1) or chemoradiation for high-risk features (positive margin, positive nodes).

by_intent.patient_group: Unresectable perihilar CCA meeting transplant criteria (≤3 cm radial diameter, no metastases, no nodal disease)
by_intent.approach: Liver transplantation per UNOS-approved protocol; consider neoadjuvant chemoradiation before transplant.
Systemic therapy (preferred) ± targeted therapy for actionable alterations; clinical trial; locoregional therapy for intrahepatic CCA; palliative RT; best supportive care.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary evaluation is recommended for all patients with BTC, particularly for assessment of resectability in gallbladder cancer with jaundice and for intrahepatic and extrahepatic CCA. The team should include surgeons with hepatobiliary expertise, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and interventional radiologists. For extrahepatic CCA, multidisciplinary review of imaging prior to biliary drainage is essential to avoid compromising accurate staging.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is not explicitly defined in the NCCN guidelines for BTC, but eligibility for systemic therapy generally follows standard oncology criteria (ECOG 0–2). Patients with adequate organ function and acceptable performance status are candidates for combination chemotherapy with durvalumab/pembrolizumab plus gemcitabine/cisplatin. For those with poor performance status or significant comorbidities, single-agent chemotherapy (gemcitabine, capecitabine, 5-FU/leucovorin) or best supportive care may be considered.

management_pathways.title: Gallbladder Cancer: Incidental Finding of Suspicious Mass During Surgery

nodes.criteria: Hepatobiliary surgery expertise available; convincing clinical evidence of cancer; resectable

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post-surgical treatment based on pathologic stage: R0, negative nodes → systemic therapy, clinical trial, or observe; R1 or positive nodes → systemic therapy, clinical trial, or combination chemotherapy + chemoradiation (category 2B for gallbladder).

options.label: Visual inspect abdomen, document findings, refer to hepatobiliary surgeon, proceed with staging

options.recommendation: No additional surgery needed; long-term survival approaches 100%.

items.recommendation: Recommended for all BTC subsites. Includes testing for NTRK fusions, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, BRAF V600E, FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements (iCCA/eCCA), IDH1 mutations (iCCA/eCCA), HER2 overexpression/amplification, RET fusions, KRAS G12C, NRG1 fusions (iCCA/eCCA). items.recommendation: For patients with dMMR/MSI-H tumors, family history suggestive of BRCA1/2 PVs, young age, no known liver disease risk factors (BIL-B).

items.recommendation: For all sites when biopsy is needed (GALL-1 footnote f, GALL-B, INTRA-1 footnote g, EXTRA-1 footnote k). FNA may be insufficient for biomarker testing.

items.text: Biopsy not always necessary before surgery if imaging strongly suggests malignancy
items.recommendation: For gallbladder cancer and intrahepatic CCA, biopsy may be avoided if definitive resection planned. For extrahepatic CCA, biopsy only after resectability/transplant candidacy determined; transperitoneal biopsy contraindicated for transplant candidates (EXTRA-1 footnote h).

items.recommendation: For biomarker testing when tissue is scant or not available (BIL-B).

items.recommendation: Early involvement for assessment of resectability. For extrahepatic CCA, prior to biliary drainage.

items.recommendation: For patients with cirrhosis or who may qualify for transplant exception points (INTRA-1 footnote s).
