items.recommendation: To rule out metastatic adenocarcinoma from another primary (INTRA-1).

surgery.role: Complete resection with negative margins (R0) remains the only potentially curative treatment for patients with BTC. Surgery is indicated for resectable gallbladder cancer, intrahepatic CCA, and extrahepatic CCA (hilar and distal). The goal is to achieve negative margins with regional lymphadenectomy.
principles: A preoperative biopsy is not always necessary before proceeding with a definitive, potentially curative resection; a suspicious mass on imaging in the proper clinical setting should be treated as malignant.

Diagnostic laparoscopy should be considered to rule out unresectable disseminated disease, especially for gallbladder cancer (higher yield in T3+, poorly differentiated, margin-positive) and intrahepatic CCA.

Initial exploration should assess for multifocal hepatic disease, lymph node metastases, and distant metastases. Lymph node metastases beyond the porta hepatis (or beyond the head of the pancreas for distal CCA) and distant metastatic disease contraindicate resection.
Regional lymphadenectomy is a standard part of curative resections for all BTC subsites.
Minimally invasive approaches by experienced surgeons have been proven safe and effective for well-selected cases (GALL-A, INTRA-A).
For gallbladder cancer with jaundice, surgery is a relative contraindication; select patients with localized node-negative disease may benefit. Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B).

For hilar CCA, extended hepatic resection (including caudate lobe) is often necessary to achieve negative margins. Preoperative biliary drainage of the future liver remnant (FLR) and portal vein embolization should be considered for small FLR.
For distal CCA, pancreaticoduodenectomy is the standard operation. Frozen section of proximal bile duct margin is recommended if suspected diagnosis.
Combined hepatic and pancreatic resections to clear distant nodal disease (as opposed to biliary extent) are not recommended due to high morbidity without obvious survival advantage (EXTRA-A).

procedures.name: Radical cholecystectomy (gallbladder cancer)
procedures.approach: Cholecystectomy + en bloc hepatic resection (typically segments IVB and V) + portal lymphadenectomy ± bile duct excision for malignant involvement
procedures.indications: T1b or greater, T1a with positive margins, or cystic duct node positive. Also for suspicious mass on imaging or intraoperative finding.
procedures.key_complications: Bile leak, hepatic insufficiency, perioperative morbidity (53% with major hepatectomy and bile duct excision per D'Angelica et al.). Port site resection not recommended (Maker et al. 2012).

procedures.name: Hepatic resection for intrahepatic CCA
procedures.approach: Anatomic or non-anatomic hepatic resection with negative margins. Major resections often necessary; wedge/segmental resections are appropriate if negative margin achievable. Regional lymphadenectomy of porta hepatis.
procedures.indications: Resectable isolated intrahepatic mass without multifocal disease or distant metastases. In highly selected cases with limited multifocal disease or gross lymph node metastases, resection can be considered.
procedures.key_complications: Liver failure, bile leak, hemorrhage. Multifocal disease generally contraindicates resection.

procedures.name: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (for distal CCA)
procedures.approach: Standard pancreaticoduodenectomy with regional lymphadenectomy. Frozen section of proximal bile duct margin recommended.
procedures.indications: Distal extrahepatic CCA (tumors below the cystic duct junction and above the ampulla of Vater).
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic leak, delayed gastric emptying, bile leak, wound infection.

procedures.name: Major hepatic resection with caudate lobectomy (for hilar CCA)
procedures.approach: Extended hepatectomy (right or left) including caudate lobe, bile duct resection, and Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. Portal vein/hepatic artery resection/reconstruction if needed.
procedures.indications: Perihilar CCA (Klatskin tumors). Resection requires contralateral intact arterial and portal inflow and biliary drainage.
procedures.key_complications: Liver failure, bile leak, vascular complications, high morbidity. Preoperative biliary drainage of FLR and portal vein embolization may reduce risk.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by the ability to achieve a complete (R0) resection with negative margins and preserve adequate liver function. For gallbladder cancer: no distant metastases, no lymph node metastases beyond the porta hepatis (celiac or aortocaval), no extensive vascular involvement. For intrahepatic CCA: no multifocal liver disease (generally), no distant nodal metastases, no distant metastases. For hilar CCA: tumor must be resectable along with involved biliary tree and hemi-liver; contralateral liver requires intact arterial and portal inflow and biliary drainage. Unresectability includes distant metastases, peritoneal disease, extensive lymph node involvement beyond regional basins, and invasion of main portal vein or hepatic artery bilaterally (for hilar CCA).

radiation_therapy.role: RT is used for definitive treatment of unresectable disease (especially intrahepatic CCA), postoperative adjuvant treatment for extrahepatic CCA and gallbladder cancer, and palliation of symptoms from primary tumor or metastases. Brachytherapy may be considered at experienced centers (BIL-D).
principles: All tumors irrespective of location may be amenable to RT using 3D-CRT, IMRT, or SBRT. IGRT is strongly recommended to improve treatment accuracy and reduce toxicity (BIL-D).
RT dosing is based on the ability to meet normal organ constraints and underlying liver function (Apisarnthanarax et al. 2022).

Postoperative RT is an option for resected extrahepatic CCA and gallbladder cancer. Target volumes should cover draining regional lymph nodes: porta hepatis, celiac, superior mesenteric, gastrohepatic, para-aortic to 45 Gy at 1.8 Gy/fraction and tumor bed to 50–60 Gy at 1.8–2 Gy/fraction depending on margin positivity (Ben-Josef et al. 2015).
Palliative RT is appropriate for symptom control of primary tumor and metastatic lesions (bone, brain).

dose_frameworks.name: SBRT for unresectable tumors
dose_frameworks.indication: Preferred for unresectable intrahepatic CCA if dose constraints can be met. BED10 >100 Gy preferred (Bowlus et al. 2023; Apisarnthanarax et al. 2022).

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: Using photons (Tao et al. 2016) or protons (Hong et al. 2016) at experienced centers. Median EQD2 80.5 Gy.

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: If SBRT/hypofractionation not possible. Chemoradiation up to 60 Gy/30 fractions is recommended (Tao et al. 2016).

dose_frameworks.name: Postoperative conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: For resected extrahepatic CCA and gallbladder cancer. Boost to tumor bed based on margin status.

approaches.name: Definitive RT for unresectable intrahepatic CCA
approaches.dose_fractionation: SBRT 40–60 Gy/3–5 fractions or hypofractionation 58–67.5 Gy/15 fractions or conventional 60–77 Gy/30–35 fractions
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not required for SBRT; chemoradiation with 5-FU or capecitabine for conventional fractionation
approaches.indication: Unresectable intrahepatic CCA without extrahepatic disease. May be used alone or combined with other locoregional therapies.
approaches.key_trial: Tao et al. 2016: Higher doses associated with better 3-year OS (73% vs 38%, P=.017) and local control (78% vs 45%, P=.04).
approaches.toxicities: Radiation-induced liver disease, biliary stricture, gastrointestinal toxicity, fatigue.

approaches.name: Adjuvant chemoradiation for resected extrahepatic CCA and gallbladder cancer
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45 Gy/25 fractions to nodal regions + 50–60 Gy/25–33 fractions to tumor bed
approaches.indication: Post-resection for R1 margins, positive nodes, or high-risk features. SWOG S0809: 2-year OS 65%, median survival 35 months (Ben-Josef et al. 2015).
approaches.key_trial: SWOG S0809 (Ben-Josef et al. 2015). Ostwal et al. 2024: Adjuvant gemcitabine/cisplatin + chemoradiation in gallbladder cancer improved outcomes.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Varies (e.g., 30 Gy/10 fractions for bone metastases; 8 Gy single fraction)
approaches.indication: Symptom control for primary tumor or metastatic sites (bone, brain, nodal).
approaches.key_trial: None specific; extrapolated from other solid tumors.
approaches.toxicities: Dependent on site; fatigue, nausea, dermatitis.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is the cornerstone of treatment for unresectable and metastatic BTC. First-line options include immunotherapy plus chemotherapy (durvalumab or pembrolizumab with gemcitabine/cisplatin, category 1) or chemotherapy alone (gemcitabine/cisplatin, category 1). Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine (category 1) is recommended for resected disease. For progressive disease, subsequent-line options include FOLFOX (preferred), FOLFIRI, liposomal irinotecan/5-FU/LV (category 2B), regorafenib (category 2B), and targeted therapy for biomarker-selected patients. Biomarker testing (MGPT) is recommended for all unresectable/metastatic patients to identify actionable alterations (NTRK fusions, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, BRAF V600E, FGFR2 fusions, IDH1 mutations, HER2 amplification/overexpression, RET fusions, KRAS G12C, NRG1 fusions). Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab (BIL-C).

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on BILCAP trial: improved OS in per-protocol analysis (HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.58–0.97); median OS 51.1 mo vs 36.4 mo. Intent-to-treat analysis did not reach statistical significance.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine (commonly followed by chemoradiation), Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (carboplatin if cisplatin ineligible), Fluorouracil/Leucovorin, Gemcitabine alone.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: TOPAZ-1: OS HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.66–0.97), PFS HR 0.75. KEYNOTE-966: OS HR 0.83 (95% CI 0.72–0.95). Both double-blind phase III trials. Durvalumab regimen also recommended for recurrent disease >6 months after curative surgery.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine alone (category 1), Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine, Capecitabine/Gemcitabine, Capecitabine/Oxaliplatin, FOLFOX, Gemcitabine/Oxaliplatin, single agents (Capecitabine, 5-FU/LV, Gemcitabine).

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ABC-06: median OS 6.2 vs 5.3 mo (adjusted HR 0.69, P=.031) compared to active symptom control.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: FOLFIRI, Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan (category 2B), Regorafenib (category 2B), Nivolumab (category 2B for patients without prior checkpoint inhibitor). Also consider re-use of first-line regimens if >6 months since prior therapy.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Targeted therapy (first-line or subsequent-line for biomarker-selected patients)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: See BIL-C 3 of 5 for specific agents
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on phase II/III trials showing ORR and survival benefit in biomarker-defined populations.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Durvalumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 days for up to 8 cycles, then durvalumab maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, nausea, immune-related adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 days for up to 8 cycles, then pembrolizumab maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to above; immune-mediated pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine alone
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, nausea, nephrotoxicity.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 bolus then 2400 mg/m2 over 46 h

drugs.name: Pemigatinib
drugs.day: Once daily for 14 days on, 7 days off

key_regimens.name: Ivosidenib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: QT prolongation, differentiation syndrome, fatigue, nausea.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Subsequent Management
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For patients receiving neoadjuvant or primary systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic disease, reassessment every 2–3 months is reasonable (BIL-C footnote c). For adjuvant therapy, evaluate after completion of planned course (typically 6 months) and then during surveillance.
response_logic: After neoadjuvant systemic therapy (gallbladder cancer, selected intrahepatic CCA), patients should be reassessed for response. If favorable (downstaging), reconsider resection or locoregional therapy.

After primary systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic disease, assess for response. Options for responding or stable disease: continue maintenance therapy, consider resection if converted to resectable, or consider locoregional therapy (arterially directed therapies, RT).
If disease progression on or after systemic therapy, consider subsequent-line systemic therapy based on prior regimens, molecular testing results, and extent of liver dysfunction (BIL-C).

For unresectable intrahepatic CCA treated with locoregional therapy (Y90, TACE, ablation), response should be assessed using multiphasic CT/MRI with IV contrast. Consider personalized dosimetry (Y90 >205 Gy) to improve outcomes (Paz-Fumagalli et al. 2021).
imaging_recommendations: For post-surgical surveillance of gallbladder cancer, intrahepatic CCA, and extrahepatic CCA: consider multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT with or without contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated (based on BILCAP trial schedule).
For gallbladder cancer: also consider CEA and CA 19-9 as clinically indicated (GALL-6).
For intrahepatic CCA: surveillance schedule same as above (INTRA-2).
For extrahepatic CCA: updated in 2026 to specify multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT ± contrast (EXTRA-2).
There are no data to support a specific surveillance schedule; physicians should discuss appropriate follow-up schedules/imaging with patients.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If there is suspicion of recurrent or progressive disease, re-evaluate according to initial workup (GALL-6, INTRA-2, EXTRA-2).
For mixed HCC-CCA with recurrence after prior resection, consider repeat biopsy to ascertain dominant histology (HCC vs CCA component) to guide systemic therapy selection (INTRA-B).

If initial biopsy yields insufficient tumor content for biomarker testing, consider repeat biopsy depending on tumor accessibility, safety, and clinical context; ctDNA testing may also be considered (BIL-B).
For patients with resected disease who develop recurrence, management follows the same pathways as initial presentation (e.g., mass on imaging, jaundice, metastatic) (GALL-6).

For patients with unresectable/metastatic disease who progress on immunotherapy-containing regimen, subsequent-line therapy should consider excluding further checkpoint inhibitors due to lack of data (BIL-C footnote j).

prognosis.overall: Biliary tract cancers are highly lethal. For gallbladder cancer, a retrospective analysis of 435 patients showed a median overall survival (OS) of 10.3 months for the entire cohort [MS-3]. For intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, 5-year OS after resection ranges from 18% to 38% depending on risk factors [MS-10]. For extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, reported 5-year survival rates following complete resection are 20%–42% for hilar tumors and 16%–52% for distal tumors [MS-17]. Stage is the strongest prognostic factor across all subsites.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated as 5-yr; median OS 12.9 months
by_stage.context: From retrospective analysis of 435 patients at Memorial Sloan Kettering [MS-3]

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated as 5-yr; median OS 5.8 months

by_stage.stage: Intrahepatic CCA Stage I (resected, N0, no vascular invasion, solitary tumor)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-yr survival higher for patients lacking all risk factors (multiple tumors, vascular invasion, N1) vs those with ≥1 risk factor: 38.3%, 27.3%, 18.1% respectively
by_stage.context: Multi-institutional international study of 449 patients undergoing surgery [MS-10]

prognostic_factors: Tumor stage (T and N) is the strongest prognostic factor for gallbladder cancer [MS-3]
Lymph node metastasis is an independent predictor of survival for intrahepatic and extrahepatic CCA [MS-10, MS-17]
Margin status (R0 vs R1 vs R2) strongly predicts outcome [MS-12]
Jaundice in gallbladder cancer predicts advanced disease and poor prognosis (disease-specific survival 6 vs 16 months; P<.0001) [MS-4]
For intrahepatic CCA, multifocal disease, vascular invasion, and lymph node metastasis are independent adverse factors [MS-13]
For perihilar CCA, T stage according to Blumgart system correlates with R0 resection rate and median survival [MS-11]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Overall prognosis remains poor due to frequent late diagnosis and high recurrence rates. For gallbladder cancer, the median survival for advanced disease is about 10 months with modern therapy. For intrahepatic CCA, recurrence rates are high: 44%, 65%, and 70% at 1, 3, and 5 years post-resection [MS-19]. Five-year OS for intrahepatic CCA is approximately 30% for resected patients but <5% for advanced disease.

disease_related.management: Biliary drainage (ERCP/PTC); multidisciplinary planning; drainage of >50% viable liver volume including future liver remnant; avoid draining atrophic segments [BIL-A]
Antibiotics; biliary drainage; if associated with stenting, consider stent revision
Multidisciplinary evaluation; cholangiography (MRCP preferred); consider ERCP/PTC for therapeutic intervention [BIL-A]

disease_related.complication: Liver failure (in setting of cirrhosis or extensive tumor)
disease_related.management: Assess hepatic reserve; consider transplant evaluation for eligible patients; palliative care

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia) – grade 3/4 neutropenia in 19%–25% of patients receiving gemcitabine/cisplatin [MS-22]
Neuropathy (from oxaliplatin, paclitaxel) – manage with dose reduction, supportive care
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) – refer to NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [BIL-C 2]
targeted_therapy: FGFR inhibitors (pemigatinib, futibatinib, erdafitinib): hyperphosphatemia, nail toxicity, stomatitis, fatigue, ocular toxicity [MS-29]
IDH1 inhibitor (ivosidenib): ascites, QTc prolongation, fatigue [MS-30]
HER2-directed therapy (trastuzumab deruxtecan): interstitial lung disease, nausea, fatigue [MS-30]
BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib): pyrexia, rash, fatigue, arthralgia
TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib): dizziness, fatigue, weight gain
surgery: Postoperative morbidity – bile leak, hepatic insufficiency, infection, bleeding
For gallbladder cancer: bile duct resection increases morbidity (60% in one series) without survival benefit [MS-5]
For hilar CCA: major hepatic resection with caudate lobectomy; portal vein/hepatic artery reconstruction may be needed, carries higher morbidity
Combined hepatic and pancreatic resections are highly morbid; only for very select healthy patients [MS-17]
radiation: Radiation-induced liver disease (RILD) – risk depends on liver function and dose constraints [BIL-D]
Biliary stricture, duodenal/gastric toxicity (with SBRT or hypofractionation)

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral to the management of biliary tract cancers, especially given the high symptom burden from obstructive jaundice, pain, and cachexia. The NCCN Guidelines reference the NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care and the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List including social determinants of health [GALL-1, INTRA-1, EXTRA-1]. Best supportive care is listed as an option for patients with unresectable or metastatic disease who are not candidates for active therapy.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Biliary obstruction often leads to malabsorption, steatorrhea, and weight loss. Patients with jaundice may require biliary drainage before systemic therapy to improve nutritional status. Pancreatic enzyme replacement may be indicated if pancreatic duct obstruction occurs. Routine nutritional assessment is recommended, and referral to a dietitian should be considered, though specific recommendations are not detailed in this guideline.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Standard prophylactic antiemetic therapy appropriate for moderately emetogenic chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine/cisplatin) should include a 5-HT3 antagonist, dexamethasone, and NK1 antagonist. The guideline does not provide a specific antiemetic protocol but references management of immune checkpoint inhibitor toxicities. For targeted therapies, antiemetic prophylaxis should be tailored to the specific agent's emetogenic risk.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: The guideline does not provide specific guidance on GCSF use. Standard practice for primary prophylaxis is indicated when febrile neutropenia risk is >20%. The ABC-02 trial reported grade 3/4 neutropenia in 25% of patients receiving gemcitabine/cisplatin but no significant difference in neutropenic infections [MS-23]. Clinical judgment and patient-specific factors should guide GCSF use.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: The guideline does not specifically address VTE prophylaxis. Biliary tract cancer patients are at moderate to high risk for venous thromboembolism. Standard pharmacologic prophylaxis is recommended for hospitalized patients with cancer. For outpatients, consider prophylaxis if high risk (e.g., Khorana score ≥2) and no contraindications. Biliary drainage devices may increase infection risk.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain is common due to hepatic capsule distension, biliary obstruction, or peritoneal metastases. Management includes analgesics per WHO ladder, celiac plexus block for refractory pain, and palliative radiation for symptomatic metastases (bone, brain). Biliary drainage can alleviate pain from biliary obstruction. Refer to NCCN Adult Cancer Pain Guidelines for detailed management.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: The NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List should be utilized throughout the care continuum [GALL-1, INTRA-1, EXTRA-1]. Social determinants of health should be assessed. Patients may benefit from psychological counseling, support groups (e.g., Cholangiocarcinoma Foundation), and social work services given the high emotional burden of this aggressive disease.
supportive_care.dental_care: The guideline does not provide specific dental care recommendations. Standard oncology dental care should be provided, including oral assessment before initiating chemotherapy (especially fluoropyrimidines which can cause mucositis) and before radiation to the upper abdomen (though relatively low oral toxicity). Bisphosphonate or RANKL inhibitor use is not standard in this disease, but if used (e.g., for bone metastases), dental evaluation is needed to prevent osteonecrosis of the jaw.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: There are no data to support a specific surveillance schedule or tests; physicians should discuss appropriate follow-up schedules/imaging with patients [GALL-6, INTRA-2, EXTRA-2]. For gallbladder cancer, consider imaging (multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT ± contrast) every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated [GALL-6]. Consider CEA and CA 19-9 as clinically indicated. For intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, consider multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT with contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years [INTRA-2]. For extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, consider multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT ± contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years [EXTRA-2]. The surveillance schedule is based on the phase III BILCAP trial [GALL-6, INTRA-2, EXTRA-2].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Given the high risk of recurrence (approximately 35% for gallbladder cancer with median time to recurrence 9.5 months; 44% at 1 year for intrahepatic CCA), regular surveillance aims to detect recurrent disease early enough to consider salvage therapy. Imaging recommendations follow the schedule used in the BILCAP trial, which provides a reasonable framework although no data prove survival benefit from surveillance. The patterns of recurrence are predominantly distant (for gallbladder, distant sites are most common; for intrahepatic CCA, both intrahepatic and extrahepatic recurrences occur).
late_effects_screening: For patients who received chemotherapy: monitoring for persistent neuropathy (platinum, taxane), renal dysfunction (cisplatin), and myelodysplasia (rare, after prolonged chemotherapy).

For patients who received radiation: liver function tests and imaging for radiation-induced liver disease; consider screening for biliary strictures, duodenal ulceration, and portal hypertension if high-dose RT to the hilum was used.
For patients who underwent major hepatic resection: monitor for hepatic insufficiency, portal hypertension, and need for long-term hepatology follow-up.
For patients who received immunotherapy: monitor for late immune-related adverse events (endocrine dysfunction, pneumonitis, arthritis) which can occur months after treatment cessation.
For targeted therapy: FGFR inhibitors may cause persistent nail changes, ocular toxicity (dry eye, retinal detachment); HER2-targeted ADC may cause late interstitial lung disease.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence is common after curative-intent resection. In gallbladder cancer, a study of 217 patients reported 35.0% recurrence with median time to recurrence 9.5 months; most recurrences occur within 2 years post-surgery [MS-8]. Common sites include liver, peritoneum, lymph nodes, and distant metastases. For intrahepatic CCA, cumulative recurrence rates are 44%, 65%, and 70% at 1, 3, and 5 years post-resection; most occur within 5 years with highest risk in the first 2 years [MS-19]. For extrahepatic CCA, recurrence rates are also high; in one series of 80 patients, 48.8% died of disease by 28 months [MS-19].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History and physical examination at each follow-up visit, frequency aligned with imaging schedule.
For gallbladder cancer: Consider imaging every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated [GALL-6].

For intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma: Consider multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT with contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated [INTRA-2].

For extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma: Consider multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT ± contrast every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated [EXTRA-2].
Surveillance beyond 5 years is not standardized; long-term follow-up per clinical judgment.
imaging_strategy: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast: preferred for follow-up imaging per NCCN Principles of Imaging [BIL-A].
Chest CT with or without contrast: recommended for detection of pulmonary metastases.
PET/CT has limited sensitivity but high specificity and may be used when there is equivocal finding or on a case-by-case basis; routine use not proven in prospective trials [BIL-A].
For intrahepatic CCA, delayed phase imaging is preferred when the diagnosis is suspected or confirmed [BIL-A].
No specific data to support a particular modality over another; choice based on institutional practice and patient factors.
laboratory_monitoring: CEA and CA 19-9: Consider as clinically indicated; these are baseline tests and should not be done to confirm diagnosis [GALL-4, GALL-5, INTRA-1, EXTRA-1].
Liver function tests (LFTs): periodic monitoring for hepatic dysfunction.
No evidence to support a specific frequency for laboratory testing; clinical judgment based on patient status.
supportive_follow_up: Refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List for psychosocial assessment throughout care continuum [GALL-1, INTRA-1, EXTRA-1].
Multidisciplinary re-evaluation in the event of disease relapse or progression per initial workup pathways.
Consider referral to palliative care for symptom management as needed.

special_situations.key_message: Estimated 1%–10% of primary liver tumors. Molecular profiling recommended. Standard treatment for resectable disease is liver resection. For advanced disease, gemcitabine/cisplatin plus immunotherapy (durvalumab or pembrolizumab) is appropriate first-line therapy.
special_situations.management: Resection for resectable mixed HCC-CCA. For unresectable limited hepatic disease, consider locoregional therapies (similar to HCC and iCCA). For advanced disease requiring systemic therapy, cisplatin/gemcitabine + durvalumab or pembrolizumab is recommended. At progression, consider molecularly targeted therapy if targetable aberration identified. If no target, consider nivolumab + ipilimumab or regorafenib. Repeat biopsy may be warranted to reassess dominant histology at progression.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective series of 101 patients showed trend towards longer median OS with chemotherapy vs non-chemotherapy (15.5 vs 5.3 months; P=.052) [MS-16].

special_situations.key_message: Jaundice portends a poor prognosis but select patients may be candidates for curative-intent resection. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy should be considered.
special_situations.management: Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B) for locoregionally advanced disease to identify patients most likely to benefit from surgery. Only a rare group of patients with localized node-negative disease may benefit from complete resection. Biliary drainage should be considered before resection and systemic therapy. Decisions should be made by multidisciplinary team.
special_situations.evidence: Resectability rate low (7%) in patients presenting with jaundice; even fewer with negative margins (5%) and no disease-free survivors at 2 years [MS-4]. Retrospective analysis of 74 patients with locally advanced/lymph-node positive disease who received systemic therapy: 30% underwent resection, OS significantly greater for those who had definitive resection (51 vs 11 months; P=.003) [MS-7].

special_situations.key_message: Very highly selected candidates may be considered for liver transplantation. Eligibility criteria for transplant exception points: biopsy-proven iCCA or mixed HCC-iCCA, presence of cirrhosis, unresectable, received locoregional or systemic therapy, and 6 months from diagnosis or last treatment with no new lesions or extrahepatic disease.
special_situations.management: Referral to hepatologist and transplant center with UNOS-approved protocol. Patients with very early iCCA (single tumors ≤2 cm) have 65% 5-year actuarial survival after transplant in retrospective studies.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective study: very early iCCA (single ≤2 cm) had 5-year cumulative risk of recurrence 18% vs 61% for advanced iCCA [MS-13]. Liver transplantation associated with improved prognosis vs resection in unmatched (HR 0.65; P=.002) and matched (HR 0.62; P=.009) cohorts [MS-13].

special_situations.key_message: Potentially curative for selected patients with lymph node-negative, non-disseminated, locally advanced hilar CCA (tumor ≤3 cm radial diameter, no intrahepatic/extahepatic metastases, no nodal disease). Especially considered for patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis.
special_situations.management: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by liver transplantation under UNOS-approved protocol. Transperitoneal biopsy is contraindicated in transplant candidates.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective evidence suggests neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by transplant is effective; results from 12 US centers showed efficacy [MS-17]. Combined liver transplantation plus neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemoradiation associated with higher RFS than resection in some studies [MS-17].

special_situations.key_message: Limited clinical trial data exist; no standard regimen defined. Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for locoregionally advanced disease to rule out rapid progression and avoid futile surgery.
special_situations.management: Individualized decision in close consultation with surgical oncologist and multidisciplinary team. A period of 2–6 months with reassessment every 2–3 months is reasonable. Regimens same as for advanced disease. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation not currently recommended (but prospective study showed R0 in 14/28 patients, 5-year OS 47%).
special_situations.evidence: Systematic review of 8 studies: ~33% of 474 patients achieved R0 resection with neoadjuvant therapy [MS-7]. Phase III POLCAGB trial underway [MS-7].

special_situations.situation: Germline testing in biliary tract cancers
special_situations.key_message: Up to 9%–11% of BTCs harbor germline pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants, including BRCA2, BRCA1, MLH1, MSH2, PALB2, RAD51D, BAP1, ATM. Consider genetic counseling referral and potential germline testing in patients with young age, strong personal/family cancer history, no known risk factors for liver disease, or somatic mutations suspected to be germline.
special_situations.management: Insufficient evidence for universal germline testing. For patients with known germline PV, insufficient evidence to support screening for biliary tract malignancies.
special_situations.evidence: Studies in large cohorts: germline P/LP variants in 9%–11% of BTCs, highest prevalence in BRCA2 [MS-25].

special_situations.key_message: Consider serum IgG4 testing in patients with extrahepatic biliary strictures where diagnosis is not clear, to avoid unnecessary surgical resection.
special_situations.management: Refer to expert center if IgG4-related cholangiopathy confirmed.
special_situations.evidence: Patients with IgG4-related cholangiopathy should be referred to expert center [EXTRA-1].

clinical_pearls: Complete resection with negative margins remains the only potentially curative treatment for biliary tract cancers; for gallbladder cancer, stage T1a with negative margins can be observed after simple cholecystectomy, with long-term survival approaching 100% [MS-5].

The combination of gemcitabine/cisplatin plus either durvalumab (TOPAZ-1) or pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-966) is now the standard first-line therapy for unresectable or metastatic BTC, both category 1 preferred recommendations. Durvalumab/gemcitabine/cisplatin showed OS HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.66–0.97; P=.021) and pembrolizumab/gemcitabine/cisplatin showed OS HR 0.83 (95% CI 0.72–0.95; P=.0034) [MS-22].

Adjuvant capecitabine (category 1) improves overall survival in resected BTC based on the BILCAP trial: median OS 51.1 months vs 36.4 months in per-protocol analysis (HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.58–0.97; P=.028) [MS-19].

Biomarker testing (MGPT) is essential for all patients with unresectable or metastatic BTC. Key actionable alterations include FGFR2 fusions (9%–15% iCCA), IDH1 mutations (10%–20% iCCA), HER2 amplification (5%–30% depending on subsite), BRAF V600E (1%–5%), MSI-H/dMMR (1%–3%), TMB-H (<5%), and rare fusions (NTRK, RET, NRG1) [BIL-B].

For intrahepatic CCA with FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements, pemigatinib and futibatinib are preferred options over erdafitinib in the subsequent-line setting. Futibatinib showed ORR 42% (95% CI 32%–52%) in FOENIX-CA2 [MS-29].
Ivosidenib is category 1 for IDH1-mutated CCA after progression on standard therapy; ClarIDHy trial showed PFS HR 0.37 (P<.0001) and OS benefit after crossover adjustment (HR 0.49; P<.001) [MS-30].

HER2-targeted therapy is now effective in BTC: fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan for IHC3+ (ORR 56.3% in BTC subgroup of DESTINY-PanTumor02), pertuzumab + trastuzumab, tucatinib + trastuzumab, and zanidatamab-hrii are options [MS-30, MS-31].

Staging laparoscopy is recommended for patients with gallbladder mass and for intrahepatic CCA prior to definitive resection; it identifies radiographically occult disseminated disease in approximately 25% of locally advanced gallbladder cancer and reduces unnecessary laparotomy [MS-6].

For resectable hilar cholangiocarcinoma, the basic principle is complete resection with negative margins and regional lymphadenectomy, generally requiring a major hepatic resection plus caudate lobectomy. Preoperative biliary drainage of the future liver remnant and contralateral portal vein embolization should be considered for small FLR [EXTRA-A].
The NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List should be incorporated at every stage of care to address psychosocial needs and social determinants of health [GALL-1, INTRA-1, EXTRA-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Biliary Tract Cancers (Version 1.2026)
guidelines_resources.notes: Covers gallbladder cancer, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, and mixed HCC-CCA. Includes principles of surgery, pathology, imaging, biomarker testing, systemic therapy, and radiation therapy. Updated March 10, 2026.

key_trials.full_name: Cisplatin plus Gemcitabine versus Gemcitabine for Biliary Tract Cancer
key_trials.population: Locally advanced or metastatic CCA, gallbladder cancer, or ampullary cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS 8.0 vs 5.0 months (HR 0.63; 95% CI 0.51–0.77; P<.001). ORR not reported as primary, but higher with combination
key_trials.practice_change: Established gemcitabine/cisplatin as global standard first-line chemotherapy for advanced BTC.

key_trials.full_name: Durvalumab plus Gemcitabine and Cisplatin in Advanced Biliary Tract Cancer
key_trials.population: Unresectable or metastatic BTC with no prior systemic treatment
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 12.9 vs 11.3 months (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.64–0.91) in updated analysis. Primary analysis: HR 0.80; 95% CI 0.66–0.97; P=.021
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemoimmunotherapy as new first-line standard for advanced BTC; category 1 preferred recommendation.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab in combination with gemcitabine and cisplatin compared with gemcitabine and cisplatin alone for patients with advanced biliary tract cancer
key_trials.population: Unresectable, locally advanced, or metastatic BTC with no prior treatment
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS HR 0.86 (95% CI 0.75–1.00; P=.023) at interim analysis. ORR 29% in both groups
key_trials.practice_change: Second positive phase III trial of chemoimmunotherapy; category 1 preferred recommendation.

key_trials.full_name: Capecitabine compared with observation in resected biliary tract cancer
key_trials.population: R0/R1 resected CCA or gallbladder cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 51.1 vs 36.4 months (per-protocol HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.58–0.97; P=.028). ITT analysis: median OS 49.6 vs 36.1 months (adjusted HR 0.84; 95% CI 0.67–1.06)
key_trials.practice_change: Established capecitabine as category 1 adjuvant therapy option for resected BTC.

key_trials.population: IDH1-mutated CCA that progressed on standard chemotherapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS in ITT: median 10.3 vs 7.5 months (HR 0.79; 95% CI 0.56–1.12; P=.09). After crossover adjustment: HR 0.49 (95% CI 0.34–0.70; P<.001)
key_trials.practice_change: First targeted therapy approved for IDH1-mutated CCA; category 1 subsequent-line recommendation.

key_trials.full_name: Pemigatinib for previously treated, locally advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma
key_trials.population: Advanced, pre-treated FGFR2-fusion-positive or FGFR2-rearranged CCA
key_trials.key_result: ORR 37.0% (95% CI 27.9%–46.9%); median DOR 9.1 months (95% CI 6.0–14.5 months)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS 7.0 months (95% CI 6.1–10.5 months); median OS 17.5 months (95% CI 14.4–22.9 months)
key_trials.practice_change: FDA approval for FGFR2-fusion-positive CCA; established FGFR inhibition as standard targeted therapy.

key_trials.population: Previously unresectable or metastatic intrahepatic CCA with FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements
key_trials.key_result: ORR 42% (95% CI 32%–52%); median DOR 9.7 months (95% CI 7.6–17.0 months)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS 21.7 months (95% CI 14.5 months–NR); median PFS 9.0 months (95% CI 6.9–13.1 months); disease control rate 83% (95% CI 74%–89%)
key_trials.practice_change: FDA approval; preferred FGFR inhibitor option along with pemigatinib.

key_trials.full_name: Second-line FOLFOX chemotherapy versus active symptom control for advanced biliary tract cancer
key_trials.population: Advanced BTC previously treated with gemcitabine/cisplatin
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 6.2 vs 5.3 months (adjusted HR 0.69; P=.031)
key_trials.practice_change: Established FOLFOX as preferred subsequent-line therapy option.

key_trials.full_name: Gemcitabine and oxaliplatin chemotherapy or surveillance in resected biliary tract cancer
key_trials.key_result: No statistically significant difference in RFS or OS between arms (HR for RFS 0.88; 95% CI 0.62–1.25; P=.48)
key_trials.practice_change: GEMOX not recommended as standard adjuvant therapy in BTC.

key_trials.full_name: Zanidatamab for HER2-amplified, unresectable, locally advanced or metastatic biliary tract cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-amplified BTC progressed after gemcitabine-based therapy
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Confirmed ORR by independent central review in cohort 1
key_trials.key_result: ORR 41.3% (95% CI 30.4%–52.8%) in cohort 1; in IHC3+ subgroup ORR 51.6%, IHC2+ 5.6%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Disease control rate 68.8%; median DOR 12.9 months; median PFS 5.5 months
key_trials.practice_change: Established zanidatamab as option for HER2-positive BTC.

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy and safety of trastuzumab deruxtecan in patients with HER2-expressing solid tumors
key_trials.population: Locally advanced or metastatic HER2-overexpressing solid tumors after prior treatment
key_trials.key_result: BTC subgroup (IHC3+): ORR 56.3%; median OS 12.4 months; median PFS 7.4 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Overall population ORR 61.3% (95% CI 49.4%–72.4%) for IHC3+
key_trials.practice_change: Approved for HER2-positive BTC (IHC3+) as subsequent-line therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Dabrafenib plus trametinib in patients with BRAF V600E-mutated biliary tract cancer
key_trials.population: BRAF V600E-mutated BTC previously treated with systemic therapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS 9 months (95% CI 5–10 months); median OS 14 months (95% CI 10–33 months)
key_trials.practice_change: Approved for BRAF V600E-mutated advanced solid tumors; recommendation for BTC.
